persona.name: Jordan Rivera

occupation.title: Barista and Environmental Studies Student
occupation.organization: Stumptown Coffee Roasters / Portland State University
occupation.description: Works 25 hours a week pulling espresso shots at Stumptown while finishing a B.A. in Environmental Studies. Passionate about sustainability in all forms, which extends to their approach to beauty and skincare. Juggles coursework, work shifts, and activism.

persona.education: Currently a junior at Portland State University studying Environmental Studies with a focus on corporate sustainability. Active in the campus environmental justice coalition. Plans to graduate Spring 2027.
long_term_goals: Work in sustainability consulting for beauty or consumer goods companies
Build a zero-waste skincare routine that actually works for sensitive skin
Reduce their overall consumption footprint by 50%
Help make clean beauty more accessible and less elitist
persona.style: Jordan is thoughtful and deliberate in conversation, often pausing to find the right word. They dress in thrifted layers — oversized flannels, Carhartt beanies, Doc Martens. They have a small septum piercing and keep their hair in a short, low-maintenance cut. Their communication leans earnest and idealistic, sometimes coming across as preachy to people who don't share their values. They speak softly but with conviction.

traits: Deeply values-driven — ethics guide every purchase decision
Empathetic and sensitive to others' experiences
Can be rigid about sustainability standards, even when impractical
Creative thinker who sees connections between systems
Anxious about the state of the environment and the future
Non-confrontational but will advocate firmly for their beliefs in writing

interests: Ceramics and pottery (takes a community class)
Reading about greenwashing and corporate accountability
likes: Brands with transparent supply chains and B Corp certification
Products that are cruelty-free, vegan, and fragrance-free
Local Portland makers and small-batch skincare
When brands are honest about what 'clean' actually means
The smell of fresh coffee at work
dislikes: Brands that virtue-signal without actual policy changes
The beauty industry's pressure to look 'perfect'
Products with long ingredient lists full of unpronounceable synthetics
Fast fashion and fast beauty culture

beliefs: Personal care choices are political and environmental statements
The beauty industry needs radical transparency, not better marketing
Clean beauty shouldn't be a luxury — it should be the default
Gender is a spectrum and beauty products should reflect that
skills: Evaluating corporate sustainability claims and certifications
Making simple DIY skincare (oil cleansing, oat masks)

general: Checks EWG and Think Dirty ratings before buying any product
Prefers to buy from local makers at Portland Saturday Market
Brings their own containers when buying refillable products
Will spend 30 minutes reading a brand's sustainability page before purchasing

morning: Rinse face with water only (no morning cleanser)
Squalane oil and a simple mineral sunscreen
Oat milk latte made at home with a hand frother
Bike commute to campus or Stumptown
workday: Morning shift at Stumptown — busy weekend rushes
Study sessions at the PSU library
Quick snack from the co-op between obligations
evening: Gentle cleanser (currently using a local Portland brand's bar cleanser)
Rosehip seed oil and a simple moisturizer
Cooks a plant-based dinner — usually a grain bowl or soup
weekend: Portland Saturday Market for local skincare finds
Hiking in Forest Park or the Gorge
Meal prep and batch-making their own body oil blends

health.physical: Deals with sensitive, reactive skin that flares up from synthetic fragrances and certain preservatives. Occasional eczema patches on their hands, possibly aggravated by frequent hand-washing at the coffee shop. Active and fit from biking and hiking.
health.mental: Experiences eco-anxiety that can be overwhelming during exam periods. Therapy every other week helps. Finds time in nature and creative pursuits (ceramics, journaling) grounding.

relationships.name: Carmen Rivera
relationships.description: Puerto Rican-American nurse in Salem, OR. Supportive of Jordan's identity and values but worries about their financial stability with a humanities degree.

relationships.name: Mike Rivera
relationships.description: Electrician. More traditional in outlook. He and Jordan have a loving but sometimes strained relationship around Jordan's non-binary identity. Getting better over time.

relationships.name: Sam Ito
relationships.description: Non-binary graphic design student at PNCA. They've been together for a year and share an apartment. Sam designs labels for a local skincare maker, which is how Jordan discovered small-batch products.

relationships.name: Terrace Collective
relationships.description: A loose group of 5-6 friends from the environmental justice coalition. They share product recommendations, organize mutual aid events, and hold each other accountable on sustainability goals.
